model_narrative: We must purchase a required total number of units from a group of vendors, each of whom has submitted a bid in the form of a piecewise-linear cost function of the quantity bought, broken into consecutive segments. For each vendor we decide which single segment of its bid to operate on, how far into that chosen segment to travel, and from these the quantity bought and the cost incurred from that vendor. The objective is to minimize the total purchase cost across all vendors.

sets.name: v
sets.doc: the set of vendors who have submitted bids

sets.name: seg
sets.doc: the set of valid (vendor, segment) pairs; each vendor's cost is a piecewise-linear function of the units purchased, broken into consecutive segments, and this set lists exactly which segments exist for each vendor; segment 0 of every vendor is the 'no deal' segment with zero length representing buying nothing from that vendor

params.name: req
params.doc: the total number of units that must be purchased across all vendors, a single scalar

params.name: px
params.doc: the start x-coordinate of each vendor-segment, that is the quantity at the beginning of the segment (its q-min), in units

params.name: py
params.doc: the start y-coordinate of each vendor-segment, that is the base cost already accumulated at the beginning of the segment, in dollars

params.name: pl
params.doc: the length of each vendor-segment, that is how many additional units can be bought while travelling within the segment (its q-max minus q-min), in units; the no-deal segment has length zero

params.name: pg
params.doc: the slope of each vendor-segment, that is the marginal unit price charged for each additional unit bought while travelling within the segment, in dollars per unit

vars.name: cost
vars.doc: the total purchase cost across all vendors, in dollars

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the number of units purchased from each vendor

vars.name: y
vars.doc: the cost of the units purchased from each vendor, in dollars

vars.name: segx
vars.doc: the distance travelled into a vendor-segment measured from the segment's start point, in units; it is positive only for the segment chosen as active for that vendor

vars.name: segb
vars.doc: the segment-selection indicator; equals 1 if the segment is the active one chosen for its vendor and 0 otherwise

constraints.name: segx[v,
constraints.intent: For every vendor-segment, the distance travelled into the segment cannot exceed the length of that segment, and it is allowed to be positive only when that segment is the one selected as active for its vendor. When a segment is not selected, no travel into it is permitted.

constraints.name: segb[v,
constraints.intent: For each vendor, exactly one of that vendor's segments must be selected as the active one. Across all of a vendor's segments the selection indicators must add up to one.

constraints.name: x[vv]
constraints.intent: For each vendor, the number of units purchased is determined by which segment is active and how far into it we travel. It equals the start quantity of the selected segment plus the distance travelled into the active segment, summed over the vendor's segments.

constraints.name: y[vv]
constraints.intent: For each vendor, the cost of the units purchased is determined by which segment is active and how far into it we travel. It equals the base cost at the start of the selected segment plus the marginal unit price of the segment times the distance travelled into it, summed over the vendor's segments.

constraints.name: req
constraints.intent: The total number of units purchased across all vendors must exactly meet the required amount.

constraints.name: cost
constraints.intent: The total purchase cost is the sum of the costs of the units purchased from each vendor.

query: Vendors A and C are our two lowest bidders and the plan leans almost the whole order onto that pair. We're evaluating a concentration guardrail that holds their combined award to no more than 180,000 units so the rest of the panel stays in play. What total procurement cost does the optimizer return under that combined ceiling?

query: There's a board-level proposal to avoid having both lead suppliers locked onto their headline volume tiers at the same time — the worry is that running A's full tier and C's full tier together leaves us with no fallback if either stumbles. Suppose at most one of those two top tiers can be switched on. What does the optimizer come back with for total cost?

query: Risk management is floating a single-source ceiling that would apply uniformly to the whole panel: no individual vendor's award may exceed 140,000 units this cycle. We want to see how that across-the-board cap reshapes the bill — what total cost does the optimizer report?

query: We're stress-testing a two-part sourcing mandate that would land together this cycle: hold vendor C's award to at most 130,000 units, and at the same time commit at least 40,000 units to vendor B to stand up a second strategic source. Run both clauses at once and tell me where the total procurement bill ends up.

query: Vendor E is carrying real volume while vendor C swallows the lion's share, and E's pricing isn't far off C's. Shouldn't E be picking up at least half of whatever C is awarded, instead of the plan piling it onto one supplier? What's the model seeing that justifies leaning on C that hard?

query: Vendors B and D both showed up with bids and both get shut out completely. With two suppliers sitting idle, doesn't it make sense to keep at least 60,000 units of the order flowing between them so we're not single-threaded on the cheap bidders? What's pulling the optimizer away from using either of them?

query: Every vendor on the panel put in the effort to bid, yet a couple of them walk away with nothing at all. Wouldn't it be fairer — and safer for supply continuity — to guarantee each vendor at least a 10,000-unit token award? What's the tradeoff the optimizer is making by zeroing some of them out?

query: The plan switches vendor C's headline volume tier on and still leaves vendor B dark. If we're committing to C at that scale, shouldn't opening B's first pricing tier alongside it be the natural hedge — you don't run your biggest single award without a live backup? What's the model seeing that keeps B switched off even when C is maxed?
